Please note that payments not made directly from the Decedent / estate should not be listed in the disbursements schedule (if reimbursements were made by the estate for costs paid by third parties, the disbursement schedule must provide the date of reimbursement, the sum reimbursed, the reimbursed individuals name, and a separate schedule itemizing all costs). 2) Please itemize all costs for "Funeral for decedent" totaling $10,710.06 3) Please explain 10/3/19 "Filing fees - Letters" as there are no filing fees. Is this referring to certified copy fees? 4) Petitioner alleges that a major asset of the estate is an interest in real property in Pasadena, which real property has multiple individuals on title who, along with Decedent, have died. If Decedent was on the title, please allege why his interest has not already been listed in an I&A and why his interest needs to be determined in the LA County proceeding. Please also allege how rent and expenses were determined (which is alleged to have been for Decedent's 1/3 interest). Please also allege the status of the proceeding in Los Angeles and why 1 year is needed to close this estate. 5) Please provide further information on the equity line of credit, including who applied for the line of credit, what was purchased with the line of credit, whether any amounts were withdrawn after Decedent's death, and the current balance. Please include a copy of the latest statement. Please also itemize each payment, including all information requested in the issue above. Please also explain how payments were made by Decedent / estate after Decedent's death and prior to Administrator's letters being issued (7/2018 to 10/2/19). 6) Please provide explanation of "Miscellaneous items" in the amount of $1,103.47. 7) Fair Market Value schedule of assets of hand as of the end of account has not been provided. rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF SU HWA WAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Court Records
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Petitioner and Administrator Andrea Leung's Motion to Seal Court Records, pursuant to C.R.C. rules 2.550 and 2.551. Specifically, the Motion seeks to seal a portion of the Settlement Agreement parties have reached in this matter to resolve the disputes related to the beneficiary designation of Decedent's Fidelity accounts. The parties are also currently petitioning for court approval of the Settlement Agreement. The Motion to Seal Court Records is DENIED. A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish all of the following: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(1)-(5); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218 ("NBC Subsidiary").) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.) First Amendment principles require that substantive courtroom proceedings in ordinary civil cases are "presumptively open." (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(c) [court records presumed to be open].) The public's right to access to court records is fundamental to our constitutional system. (See NBC Subsidiary, supra, 20 Cal.4th 1216-1217.) The right to access is heightened in probate proceedings, where broad notice and opportunity to object is fundamental. (See, e.g., Estate of Stevenson (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 1074, 1087-88 ["Notice is the cornerstone of estate proceedings. [Citations.]"].) An open discussion assumes access to the record. Notice is lacking when access to the document submitted for judicial approval is withheld from interested parties. The Motion states, "Ast [sic] the time of her death, Ms. Wan was the owner of accounts held with Fidelity Investments valued at approximately $6.9 million. A dispute arose between Petitioners and Fidelity Investments (through three separate business entities) and Fidelity Investments Charitable Gift Fund regarding a purported beneficiary designation made by decedent during the final years of her life in favor of 'Fidelity Charitable' (also known as Fidelity Investments Charitable Gift Fund)." (Motion at 2:11-16.)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF SU HWA WAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Court Records (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] The Motion further states the proposed redacted portion of the Settlement Agreement "reflect[s] only the parties' specific agreement with respect to final disposition of the disputed assets" and "include[s] the terms specifying the division of disputed assets" (Motion at 3:13-14, 20-21). The portion of the Settlement Agreement the parties seek to seal is under the heading "Distribution of Account and Annuity." The unredacted portions of the section indicate the redacted portions discuss Fidelity's distribution of the disputed assets held in the subject accounts. The Motion does not set forth specific facts in support of the court's finding that all five required statutory elements have been established. As explained in Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (2003) 110 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1283, In terms of the overriding interest requirement of a closure or sealing order, NBC Subsidiary identifies two separate elements. The first element requires the identification of an overriding interest. [Citations] Defendant has identified such a potential overriding interest-a binding contractual agreement not to disclose. The second element of the overriding interest analysis is there must be a substantial probability that it will be prejudiced absent closure or sealing. The Motion has not presented specific facts showing that the parties' privacy interests override the public interest of access to court records. The Motion also has not shown with sufficient specificity a substantial probability exists that an overriding interest will likely be prejudiced if the record is not sealed. The underlying Petition for Order Approving Settlement Agreement and Authorizing and Instructing Parties to Carry Out Its Terms is brought under Probate Code section 9837. Under Section 9837(b), "The petition [for an order authorizing a settlement] shall show the terms of the [] settlement [] and its advantage to the estate." If the parties want the court to consider approving the instant Settlement Agreement, and finding the distribution terms are to the advantage of the estate, the parties must accept that the court may require that the terms of distribution be made available on the public record to allow fulsome review and broad participation in the adjudicative process. (See Estate of Black (1976) 65 Cal.App.3d 112, 116.) The estate's financial details have been and will be published on the public record as part of administration of the estate (see, i.e., Prob. C. secs. 1060 et seq, 8850, 8901, 10901, 10951, 11603). Further, this Motion, the underlying Petition, and future required petitions and orders regarding accountings and distribution of estate assets, will be discussed at noticed public hearings; meaning, the material terms of the portion of the Settlement Agreement parties seek to seal will soon be a matter of public record as a necessary function of the court's review and ruling on the matters the parties have put before it. Both the Motion and the underlying Petition state the value of the disputed accounts at issue in the Settlement.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF SU HWA WAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Court Records (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] The Motion's argument that public disclosure of the terms of the Settlement leaves the estate and petitioner subject to "predatory [] claims or demands" and the parties potentially subject to "improper or unwarranted claims or demands, and have an impact on future, unrelated settlement or litigation" (Motion at 5:22, 6:7-9) is vague and theoretical and lacks the required specificity of substantially probable harm to warrant sealing the material. The Motion does not specify Fidelity's privacy interests beyond "the confidentiality interests of Respondents with respect to their internal practices at issue, dealings with Petitioners in particular, and the manner in which they have agreed to resolve this dispute over an account beneficiary designation" and "confidential financial matters and business practices." (Motion at 4:20-22, 5:24.) Again, the Motion does not present specificity as to (1) why these interests rise to the level of overriding the right of public access to court records, or (2) what substantially likely harm would befall these interests if the material is not sealed. The Motion also argues the parties' agreement in the Settlement not to disclose its terms is itself a sufficient overriding interest. The existence of an agreement between the parties is not alone sufficient to permit sealing court documents. Whether an overriding interest exists over a contractual agreement depends on the "content of the information at issue, the relationship of the parties, or the nature of the controversy. For example, an interest in safeguarding a trade secret may overcome a presumption of openness." (Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Ct. (2003) 110 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1280-81). In further discussing the effect of parties' agreements to seal, the Universal court concluded, "We have been unable to find any appellate court decision which construes Publicker to permit sealing of court documents merely upon the agreement of the parties without a specific showing of serious injury." (Id. at 1281-82). The Motion tacitly admits the material does not rise to the level of a trade secret. The material is part of a litigation settlement agreement between Petitioners (the estate administrator and the estate's heir) and Respondents (a financial institution) over proper ownership of certain account funds held by Decedent at her death. The Settlement Agreement states the terms upon which these funds will be distributed - presumably between the parties. The Motion makes no specific argument that the content of the information sought to be sealed, the relationship of the parties, and nature of the controversy amount to an interest sufficient to override the right of public access. Broad allegations of potential harm without specific examples or articulated reasoning are insufficient to warrant sealing court records. There is no showing in the Motion of specific serious injury absent sealing.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF SU HWA WAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Court Records (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 4] The Motion improperly cites to an unpublished trial order from another Superior Court, regarding a protective order (see, CRC Rule 8.1115). Even if this Court considered this order for guidance - it does not - it would not support the Motion's argument. There, our sister trial court found the confidential matter was not relevant to the case and "the protective order does not concern public access to trials, adjudications on the merits, or court filings related to those matters." The opposite is true here. The terms of the Settlement Agreement are the entire focus of the Court's review of the underlying Petition to approve the Settlement Agreement and will be the subject of any forthcoming order(s) on it. While the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored to just three sentences of the Settlement Agreement, the Motion has not shown that sealing is the least restrictive means to protect the parties' purported interests. The Administrator in this matter was appointed with limited powers under the Independent Administration of Estates Act, and as such may enter into a settlement of a claim without court approval, after filing a Notice of Proposed Action (see, Prob. C. sec. 10518; Cal. Prac. Guide Probate Ch. 15-G at 15:921.) Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

The first account covering a 2-year period from the date of death must be filed as an interim account. See LRSF 14.34(A). 8) Please file a proof of compliance with Probate Code section 10533(a)(1) or (2) re the estate cash fully insured in observance of the FDIC limit or held in the form of authorized investment(s). rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TIMOTHY ANDREW TALBOT
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 4/9/25. Examiner's Notes emailed on 2/25/25 with the following issues: 1) Petitioner alleges that personal property was disposed of by Young Mi Kim prior to probate. Please allege whether Petitioner tried to recover the assets as executor of the estate and why the property was not listed in the I&A. 2) Petitioner alleges that Young Mi Kim closed an account that was owned by Decedent outside of probate, which had $7,670.39 at date of death. Please allege why this asset was not included in the I&A. 3) Petitioner alleges that Visitana Vacaction Properties (Timeshares) were community property and passed to Decedent's spouse at his death. However, his Will leaves his assets to his trust. Please allege why Decedent's community property interest was not included in the I&A. 4) Petitioner alleges that the estate real property (50% interest in 369 Green St) was sold, but Petitioner also alleges in par. 20 that there were no actions taken under IAEA that required Notice of Proposed Action. Please explain. It is also alleged that the real property was transferred to Young Mi Kim. Please clarify. If it was transferred to her, it should be reflected as such in the accounting, and no loss on sale should be reflected (either it was sold for a loss or it was transferred to Young Mi Kim, not both). 5) Petitioner alleges that rejection of the creditor's claim by Bank of America ($13,802.47) was filed on 7/5/24. The Proof of Service attached to that filing does not indicate when the rejection was mailed and was not signed and dated, and it is unclear whether action on the claim is barred. 6) Please allege who the trustee of the Kim and Talbot Trust is. If it is the executor, a list of the beneficiaries of the trust and their addresses should be provided.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MANUEL MINERO AND VINCENTA MINERO LIVING TRUST, AS AMENDED MARCH 1, 2007
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: **REPOSTED 2/24/25 at 2:00 PM.** Hearing. Appearance is required. An objection to the petition is on file, and it is unclear whether the objection has been withdrawn as well. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.
